You are verifier #2, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Finding F-018: "discover_and_confirm missing duplicate hypothesis_id guard"
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py (line 108)
Description: Pass-2 #15 added claim_id guards in adjudicate_with_baseline and _require_unique_ids for pipeline. But discover_and_confirm (standalone public) was not updated. Duplicate hypothesis_ids pass without error.
PoC: discover_and_confirm(..., discover=lambda c: [locked_h, locked_h]) returns two verdicts for same hypothesis_id.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py
2. Look at the code at line 108 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Discovery runner — Firewall B (discover/confirm separation) enforced by construction.
2	
3	The mirror of :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.adjudication`. In discovery mode the data surfaces
4	candidate hypotheses; Firewall B (METHODOLOGY.md §2, ADR-0005) requires that the data used to
5	*discover* a hypothesis is not the data used to *confirm* it — otherwise discovering a pattern
6	and "testing" it on the same data proves nothing.
7	
8	The engine had the split primitive (:class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`) and a confirm step that
9	refuses discovery ids, but no composition — so the separation held only if a study wired it by
10	hand (same risk Firewall A had before ADR-0008). :func:`discover_and_confirm` makes it
11	structural: it hands the ``discover`` step a corpus containing *only* the discovery partition
12	and the ``confirm`` step a corpus containing *only* the held-out partition, so neither step can
13	accidentally use the other's data.
14	
15	Honest scope (lesson from the adjudication review, ADR-0008): the engine partitions the
16	**structural** data — units, and relations whose *both* endpoints are in the partition. It
17	does NOT carry the corpus's free-form ``metadata`` into a subset (an arbitrary mapping cannot
18	be partitioned safely — a per-unit index in metadata would otherwise leak the other partition
19	wholesale), and it cannot police free-text inside ``Unit``/``Relation.attributes`` that an
20	adapter might use to reference units outside the partition. So: the structural separation is
21	enforced; keeping per-unit data out of metadata/attribute *payloads* is the adapter's
22	obligation (the Firewall-B analogue of the corpus-content caveat in ADR-0005). And, as with
23	adjudication, this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping,
24	not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could).
25	"""
26	
27	from __future__ import annotations
28	
29	import datetime as _dt
30	from typing import Callable, Iterable, Protocol
31	
32	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
33	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
34	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
35	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
36	    TimestampAuthority,
37	    require_preregistered,
38	)
39	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
40	
41	
42	class HeldOutConfirmer(Protocol):
43	    """Study-supplied: confirm one locked, data-surfaced hypothesis on the held-out corpus."""
44	
45	    def __call__(self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, held_out: Corpus) -> Verdict: ...
46	
47	
48	def subset_corpus(corpus: Corpus, ids: Iterable[str]) -> Corpus:
49	    """Return the sub-corpus of ``corpus`` restricted to ``ids``.
50	
51	    Units not in ``ids`` are dropped; a relation is kept only if *both* endpoints are in
52	    ``ids`` (a relation to a unit outside the partition would leak that unit's existence/links
53	    into the partition).
54	
55	    Corpus-level ``metadata`` is deliberately **dropped**, not carried through: it is an
56	    arbitrary mapping the engine cannot partition safely. A per-unit index living in metadata
57	    (e.g. ``{"by_unit": {unit_id: ...}}``) would otherwise hand the discovery step the held-out
58	    partition wholesale — silently defeating Firewall B. Adapters that need partition-scoped
59	    side data must put it on the ``Unit``/``Relation`` records (which *are* partitioned), not in
60	    corpus metadata; free-text inside ``attributes`` that references foreign units remains the
61	    adapter's obligation (ADR-0005 / ADR-0008 honest-scope caveat).
62	    """
63	    keep = frozenset(ids)
64	    units = tuple(u for u in corpus.units if u.unit_id in keep)
65	    relations = tuple(r for r in corpus.relations if r.source_id in keep and r.target_id in keep)
66	    return Corpus(units=units, relations=relations)
67	
68	
69	def discover_and_confirm(
70	    corpus: Corpus,
71	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
72	    *,
73	    discover: Callable[[Corpus], Iterable[Hypothesis]],
74	    confirm: HeldOutConfirmer,
75	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
76	) -> list[Verdict]:
77	    """Run discovery then confirmation with the data separation enforced by construction.
78	
79	    1. ``discover`` is handed a corpus containing only the discovery partition and returns
80	       candidate hypotheses. They must be ``DISCOVERY``-origin and genuinely **pre-registered**
81	       before confirmation — ``require_preregistered`` checks (via ``authority``) that the proof
82	       binds each hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes confirmation.
83	       Discovering and confirming on the same data, or confirming a hypothesis whose content was
84	       changed after locking, is the double-dipping Firewall B exists to prevent.
85	    2. ``confirm`` is handed a corpus containing only the held-out partition and tests each
86	       hypothesis there; the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers.
87	
88	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
89	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
90	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default. A study's ``discover`` step locks each
91	    discovered hypothesis (e.g. via :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.
92	    lock_hypothesis`) before returning it.
93	
94	    Returns the verdicts. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl. ``PreRegistrationError``) on an
95	    empty discovery or held-out partition (split ids that select no corpus units — a vacuous
96	    test), a non-``DISCOVERY`` hypothesis, a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered
97	    before confirmation, or a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution.
98	    """
99	    discovery_corpus = subset_corpus(corpus, split.discovery_ids)
100	    if not discovery_corpus.units:
101	        # the discovery partition selects no actual corpus units — a hypothesis "discovered"
102	        # from zero data is not data-surfaced at all; it would confirm cleanly on the held-out
103	        # set and silently launder a hand-authored claim through Firewall B. Fail loud.
104	        raise FirewallViolation(
105	            "discovery partition selects no corpus units — nothing to discover from "
106	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
107	        )
108	    hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))  # sees only the discovery partition
109	
110	    held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)  # disjoint from discovery (split invariant)
111	    if not held_out.units:
112	        # the confirmation partition selects no actual corpus units — confirming on an empty
113	        # held-out set is a vacuous test (the split ids don't match the corpus). Fail loud.
114	        raise FirewallViolation(
115	            "confirmation partition selects no corpus units — nothing to confirm on "
116	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
117	        )
118	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
119	    for hypothesis in hypotheses:
120	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY:
121	            raise FirewallViolation(
122	                f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is {hypothesis.origin.value!r}; the "
123	                "discovery runner expects DISCOVERY-origin (data-surfaced) hypotheses"
124	            )
125	        # Pre-registration enforced by the runner: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content
126	        # (no post-lock content swap) and its attested lock time must precede this confirmation.
127	        require_preregistered(
128	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
129	        )
130	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
131	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
132	            raise FirewallViolation(
133	                f"verdict reports hypothesis_id {verdict.hypothesis_id!r} for hypothesis "
134	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
135	            )
136	        verdicts.append(verdict)
137	    return verdicts
138

content.text: Now let me check what the `_require_unique_ids` and related guards look like in the adjudication pipeline, to understand what guard is being compared against.

/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:199:    # within-run identity uniqueness: a repeated claim_id would be counted once per occurrence in

128	    )
129	
130	
131	def _require_unique_ids(ids: list[str], what: str) -> None:
132	    """Raise FirewallViolation if ``ids`` contains a duplicate (#138).
133	
134	    Identity (claim↔hypothesis↔verdict) must be unique within a run: a repeated id would
135	    otherwise be counted once per occurrence, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing
136	    alignment_rate / verdict accounting.
137	    """
138	    seen: set[str] = set()
139	    dupes: list[str] = []
140	    for i in ids:
141	        if i in seen:
142	            dupes.append(i)
143	        seen.add(i)
144	    if dupes:
145	        raise FirewallViolation(
146	            f"duplicate {what} within one run: {sorted(set(dupes))[:3]} — identity must be "
147	            "unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator / verdict accounting)"

content.text:
The pipeline has the guard at line 452 for `hypothesis_id`. Now let me confirm the discovery.py function does NOT have an equivalent guard before iterating.

The code at line 108 of `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py` shows:

```python
hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))  # sees only the discovery partition
```

And the loop starting at line 119 iterates over all hypotheses without any duplicate `hypothesis_id` check. By contrast, `pipeline.py` line 452 calls `_require_unique_ids([h.hypothesis_id for h in hypotheses], "hypothesis_id")` before processing.

The finding is accurate: if `discover` returns two `Hypothesis` objects with the same `hypothesis_id`, both will be processed, and two verdicts will be appended to `verdicts` for the same `hypothesis_id`. There is no guard equivalent to `_require_unique_ids` in `discover_and_confirm`.

The PoC is reproducible: a `discover` lambda returning `[locked_h, locked_h]` (same object twice, same `hypothesis_id`) would result in two verdicts for that same id, with no error raised (assuming the pre-registration check passes, which it would since both are the same pre-registered object).

input.reasoning: The code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py confirms the finding. Line 108 collects hypotheses via `hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))`, and the loop at line 119 iterates all of them without any duplicate hypothesis_id guard. By contrast, pipeline.py line 452 calls `_require_unique_ids([h.hypothesis_id for h in hypotheses], "hypothesis_id")` before processing. The discover_and_confirm function has no equivalent check. The PoC is reproducible: a discover callable returning [locked_h, locked_h] with the same hypothesis_id would pass all checks (same pre-registered object satisfies require_preregistered twice, same verdict.hypothesis_id matches both times) and append two verdicts for the same hypothesis_id to the result list. This is a genuine production-readiness concern because duplicate hypothesis_ids in the returned verdict list would distort any downstream scorecard accounting, exactly as the pipeline guard's docstring warns.
